title: MVP-BERT: Redesigning Vocabularies for Chinese BERT and Multi-Vocab Pretraining

paragraphs.text:
Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## - MVP-BERT: REDESIGNING VOCABULARIES FOR CHI - NESE BERT AND MULTI VOCAB PRETRAINING

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Despite the development of pre-trained language models (PLMs) significantly raise the performances of various Chinese natural language processing (NLP) tasks, the vocabulary for these Chinese PLMs remain to be the one provided by Google Chinese Bert Devlin et al. (2018), which is based on Chinese characters. Second, the masked language model pre-training is based on a single vocabulary, which limits its downstream task performances. In this work, we first propose a novel method, _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ , to form the vocabulary of Chinese BERT, with the help of Chinese word segmentation (CWS) and subword tokenization. Then we propose three versions of multi-vocabulary pretraining (MVP) to improve the models expressiveness. Experiments show that: (a) compared with char based vocabulary, _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ does not only improves the performances of Chinese PLMs on sentence level tasks, it can also improve efficiency; (b) MVP improves PLMs’ downstream performance, especially it can improve _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ ’s performances on sequence labeling tasks.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

The pretrained language models (PLMs) including BERT Devlin et al. (2018) and its variants Yang et al. (2019); Liu et al. (2019b) have been proven beneficial for many natural language processing (NLP) tasks, such as text classification, question answering Rajpurkar et al. (2018) and natural language inference (NLI) Bowman et al. (2015), on English, Chinese and many other languages. Despite they brings amazing improvements for Chinese NLP tasks, most of the Chinese PLMs still use the vocabulary (vocab) provided by Google Chinese Bert Devlin et al. (2018). Google Chinese Bert is a character (char) based model, since it splits the Chinese characters with blank spaces. In the pre-BERT era, a part of the literature on Chinese natural language processing (NLP) first do Chinese word segmentation (CWS) to divide text into sequences of words, and use a word based vocab in NLP models Xu et al. (2015); Zou et al. (2013). There are a lot of arguments on which vocab a Chinese NLP model should adopt.

The advantages of char based models are clear. First, Char based vocab is smaller, thus reducing the model size. Second, it does not rely on CWS, thus avoiding word segmentation error, which can directly result in performance gain in span based tasks such as named entity recognition (NER). Third, char-based models are less vulnerable to data sparsity or the presence of out-of-vocab (OOV) words, and thus less prone to over-fitting (Li et al. (2019)). However, word based model has its advantages. First, it will result in shorter sequences than char based counterparties, thus are faster. Second, words are less ambiguous, thus may be helpful for models to learn the semantic meanings of words. Third, with a word based model, exposure biases may be reduced in text generation tasks (Zhao et al. (2013)). Another branch of literature try to strike a balance between the two by combining word based embedding with character based embedding Yin et al. (2016); Dong et al. (2016).

In this article, we try to strike a balance between the char based model and word based model and provides alternative approaches for building a vocab for Chinese PLMs. In this article, there are three approaches to build a vocab for Chinese PLMs: (1) following Devlin et al. (2018), separate the Chinese characters with white spaces, and then learn a sub-word tokenizer (denote as _char_ ); (2) first segment the sentences with a CWS toolkit like jieba[1] , and then learn a sub-word tokenizer (denoted

as _seg tok_ ); (3) do CWS and kept the high-frequency words as tokens and low-frequency words will be tokenized by _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ (denoted as _seg_ ). See Figure 1 for their workflow of processing an input sentence. Note that the first one is essentially the same with the vocab of Google Chinese BERT.

Inspired by the previous work that incorporate multiple vocabularies (vocabs) or combine multiple vocabs in an natural wayYin et al. (2016); Dong et al. (2016), we also investigate a series of strategies, which we will call Multi-Vocab Pretraining (MVP) strategies. The first version of MVP is to incorporate a hierarchical structure to combine the char based vocab and word based vocab. From the viewpoint of model forward pass, the embeddings of Chinese characters are aggregated to form the vector representations of multi-gram words or tokens, which then are fed into transformer encoders, and then the word based vocab will be used in masked language model (MLM) training. We will denote this version of MVP as MVP _hier_ . Note that in MVP _hier_ , the char based vocab is built by splitting the Chinese words in the word based vocab into Chinese chars, and non-Chinese tokens are kept the same. We will denote this strategy as MVP _hier_ ( _V_ ), where _V_ is a word based vocab.

The second version of MVP (denoted as MVP _pair_ ) is to employ a pair of vocabs in MLM. Due to limited resources, in this article we only consider the pair between _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ and _char_ . MVP _pair_ is depicted in Figure 2(c). In MVP _pair_ , a sentence (or a concatenation of multiple sentences in pretraining), is processed and tokenized both in _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ and _char_ , and the two sentences are encoded by two parameter-sharing transformer encoders. Whole word masking Cui et al. (2019b) is applied for pretraining. For example, the word ” `篮球` ” (basketball) is masked. The left encoder, which is with _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ , has to predict the single masked token is ” `篮球` ”, and the right encoder has to predict ” `篮` ” and ” `球` ” for two masked tokens. MLM loss from both sides will be added with weights. With MVP _pair_ , parameter sharing enables the single vocab model to absorb information from the other vocab, thus enhancing its expressiveness. Note that after pre-training, one can either keep one of the encoder or both encoders for downstream finetuning. We will denote this strategy as MVP _pair_ ( _V_ 1 _, V_ 2 _, i_ ), where _V_ 1 and _V_ 2 are two different vocabs, _i_ = _s_ means only the encoder with _V_ 1 is kept for finetuning (single vocab model), and _i_ = _e_ means both encoders are kept (ensemble model).

The third version of MVP (denoted as MVP _obj_ ) is depicted in Figure 2(b). In MVP _obj_ , the sentence is encoded only once with a fine-grained vocab, and MLM task with that vocab is conducted. As in the figure, he word ” `喜欢` ” (like) is masked, and under the vocab of _char_ , the PLM has to predict ” `喜` ” and ” `欢` ” for the two masked tokens. As additional training objective, we will employ a more coarse-grained vocab like _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ and ask the model to use the starting token (” `喜` ”)’s representation to predict the original word under _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ . We will denote this strategy as MVP _pair_ ( _V_ 1 _, V_ 2), where _V_ 1 and _V_ 2 are a pair of vocabs and _V_ 1 is the more fine-grained one.

pretraining, the three vocab building methods and three MVP strategies are compared on a series of Chinese benchmark datasets, 4 of which are sentence classification tasks, 3 are sequence labeling tasks. The experimental results demonstrate the following take-aways: 1) directly use Chinese words as vocab ( _seg_ ) does not perform well; 2) _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ based ALBERT consistently performs better than _char_ and _seg_ on sentence classification tasks, but not sequence labeling tasks; 3) MVP strategies can help to improve a single vocab model on both types of tasks, especially it can help _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ on sequence labeling tasks; 4) MVP _pair_ ensemble are the best model, but it comes with a higher inference time; 5) MVP _obj_ help to provide a better single vocab model than MVP _pair_ .

We now summaries the following contributions in this work.

- experiments on three ways of building new vocab for Chinese BERT, and _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ , the combination of CWS and subword tokenization is novel.

- We propose 3 MVP strategies for enhancing the Chinese PLMs.

## 2 RELATED WORK

Before and since Devlin et al. (2018), a large amount of literature on pretrained language model appear and push the NLP community forward with a speed that has never been witnessed before. Peters et al. (2018) is one of the earliest PLMs that learns contextualized representations of words. GPTs Radford et al. (2018; 2019) and BERT Devlin et al. (2018) take advantages of Transformer Vaswani et al. (2017). GPTs are uni-directional and make prediction on the input text in an auto-regressive manner, and BERT is bi-directional and make prediction on the whole or part of the input text. In its core, what makes BERT so powerful are the pretraing tasks, i.e., Mask language modeling (MLM) and next sentence prediction (NSP), where the former is more important. Since

3

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

BERT, a series of improvements have been proposed. The first branch of literature improves the model architecture of BERT. ALBERT Lan et al. (2019) makes BERT more light-weighted by embedding factorization and progressive cross layer parameter sharing. Zaheer et al. (2020) improve BERT’s performance on longer sequences by employing sparser attention.

The second branch of literature improve the training of BERT. Liu et al. (2019b) stabilize and improve the training of BERT with larger corpus. More work have focused on new language pretraining tasks. ALBERT Lan et al. (2019) introduce sentence order prediction (SOP). StructBERT Wang et al. (2019) designs two novel pre-training tasks, word structural task and sentence structural task, for learning of better representations of tokens and sentences. ERNIE 2.0 Sun et al. (2019) proposes a series of pretraining tasks and applies continual learning to incorporate these tasks. ELECTRA Clark et al. (2020) has a GAN-style pretraining task for efciently utilizing all tokens in pretraining. Our work is closely related to this branch of literature by design a series of novel pretraining objective by incorporating multiple vocabularies. Our proposed tasks focus on intra-sentence contextual learning, and it can be easily incorporated with other sentence structural tasks like SOP.

Another branch of literature look into the role of words in pre-training. Although not mentioned in Devlin et al. (2018), the authors propose whole word masking in their open-source repository, which is effective for pretraining BERT. In SpanBERT Joshi et al. (2019), text spans are masked in pretraining and the learned model can substantially enhance the performances of span selection tasks. It is indicated that word segmentation is especially important for Chinese PLMs. Cui et al. (2019a) and Sun et al. (2019) both show that masking tokens in the units of natural Chinese words instead of single Chinese characters can significantly improve Chinese PLMs. In this work, compared to literature, we propose to re-design the vocabulary of the Chinese BERT by combining word segmentation and sub-word tokenizations.

## 3 OUR METHODS

In this section, we present our methods for rebuilding the vocab for Chinese PLMs, and introduce our series of MVP strategies.

## 3.1 BUILDING THE VOCABS

We investigate four work-flows to process the text inputs, each corresponding to a different vocab (or a group of vocabs) (Figure 1). We first introduce the single vocab models, _char_ , _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ and _seg_ . For char based vocab _char_ , Chinese characters in the corpus are treated as words in English and are separated with blank spaces and a sub-word tokenizer is learned. _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ requires the sentences in corpus to be segmented and a sub-word tokenizer like BPE are learned on the segmented sentences. Note that in _seg tok_ , some natural word will be splitted into pieces, but there are still many tokens that have multiple Chinese chars. Finally, _seg_ with size _N_ is built with the following procedures: a) do CWS on the corpus; b) count the words’ frequency, and add the tokens from _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ with frequency 0; c) for long tail Chinese words and non-Chinese tokens, tokenize them into subword tokens with _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ , and the words’ frequencies are added to the sub-word tokens’ frequencies; d) sort the vocab via frequency, and if the most frequent N words or tokens can cover 99.95% of the corpus, then take them as vocab. Note that some of the tokens from _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ will be dropped.

## 3.2 MULTI-VOCAB PRETRAINING (MVP)

In this subsection, we will introduce MVP, which is a derivation of MLM task by Devlin et al. (2018). MVP has three versions, MVP _hier_ , MVP _obj_ and MVP _pair_ , all of which have one thing in common, that is, they require more than one vocab to implement pre-training.

Figure 2(a) depicts the architecture of MVP _hier_ and Figure 2(a) depicts its procedure for processing input sentences. Two vocab, a fine-grained vocab _Vf_ , and a more coarse-grained vocab _Vc_ , are combined in a hierarchical way. Sequences are first tokenized via _Vc_ , and then the Chinese tokens (if containing multiple Chinese characters) are splitted into single characters. Chinese characters and non-Chinese tokens are embedded into vectors. Then representations of chars inside a word is aggregated into the representation of this word, which is further fed into the transformer encoder. During MLM task, whole word masking is applied, that is, we will mask 15% of the tokens

In MVP _obj_ , a sentence is tokenized and embedded in a fine-grained _Vf_ (e.g., a char based vocab), and MLM task on _Vf_ is conducted. However, different from the vanilla MLM, another MLM task based on a more coarse-grained vocab _Vc_ is added. For example, encoded representations of the chars ” ~~`喜`~~ ” and ” `欢` ” inside the word ” ~~`喜`~~ `欢` ” is aggregated to the vector representation of the word, and an auxiliary MLM layer is tasked to predict the word based on _Vc_ . For the aggregator in the example, we adopt BERT-style pooler, which is to use the starting token’s representation to represent the word’s representation.[4] Denote **x** _[mask]_ and **y** _[mask]_ as the masked tokens under _Vf_ and _Vc_ respectively. Now we introduce MVP _pair_ , which is the most resource-demanding version of MVP, but it will be proven beneficial. Our goal is to enhance the model with a single vocab, by introducing additional encoder with another vocab and a corresponding MLM task. In this strategy, a sentence is tokenized and embedded with two vocabs, _V_ 1 and _V_ 2, which will be fed into separate transformer encoders. Transformer encoders will share the same parameters, but the embedding layers are separate. For example in Figure 2(c), ” ~~`喜`~~ `欢` ” is masked, and thus two tokens on the left sequence and(or) one token on the right sequence are masked. The left encoder and MLM layer is tasked to recover the two tokens ” ~~`喜`~~ ” and ” `欢` ”. And on the right, the other MLM layer needs to recover ” ~~`喜`~~ `欢` ”. Through parameter sharing, self-supervised signals from one vocab is transferred to the model with the other vocab. Formally, Thus the loss function for MVP _pair_ is

If after MVP _pair_ pretraining we decide to only keep one of the encoder, we will call this model single vocab MVP _pair_ . Otherwise, we can call the model as ensemble MVP _pair_ . For single mode MVP _pair_ , finetuning is the same with vanilla ALBERT. Finetuning for ensemble mode MVP _pair_ is different. For sentence classification, the pooled vectors on both [CLS] tokens will be concatenated to be the feature vector of the classifier. When doing sequence labeling tasks, two ways of ensemble can be conducted, which we will use the example from Figure 2(c) to illustrate. The first approach is to concatenate the features from fine-grained encoder to the coarse-grained encoder. That is, representations of ” ~~`喜`~~ ” and ” `欢` ” are aggregated to the representation of ” ~~`喜`~~ `欢` ”, and it will be concatenated to the representation from the coarse-grained encoder on the right. We will call this approach fine-to-coarse ensemble. The other approach is coarse-to-fine ensemble, which is to concatenate the representation of ” ~~`喜`~~ `欢` ” from the coarse-grained encoder to ” ~~`喜`~~ ” and ” `欢` ” from the fine-grained encoder. Then the labels of ” ~~`喜`~~ ” and ” `欢` ” are predicted.

For notational convenience, we will denote the model pretrained with MVP _hier_ strategy and vocab _V_ as MVP _hier_ ( _V_ ). MVP _obj_ with a fine-grained vocab _Vf_ and a coarse-grained vocab _Vc_ are denoted

MVP _pair_ with two vocab _V_ 1 and _V_ 2 is denoted as MVP _pair_ ( _V_ 1 _, V_ 2 _, i, j_ ), where _i_ can be _single_ , meaning only keep the encoder from _V_ 1 for finetuning, or _ensemble_ , meaning keep both encoders, and _j_ can be _ftc_ (short for fine-to-coarse) or _ctf_ (coarse-to-fine), which is two approaches for finetuning MVP _pair_ on sequence labeling tasks. Note that if _i_ equals _single_ , we will neglect the parameter _j_ .

## 4 EXPERIMENTS

## 4.1 SETUP

For pre-training corpus, we use Chinese Wikipedia. The vocab size is 21128 for _char_ and _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ . 50.38% of _seg_ are Chinese tokens with length more than 1.
In this article, we adopt jieba as our CWS tool.[5] To keep 70% of the vocab as natural words from CWS, and cover 99.95% of the corpus, _seg_ is built with vocab size 69341.

For MVP _hier_ , we consider MVP _hier_ ( _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ ). For MVP _obj_ , we consider MVP _obj_ ( _char, seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ ) and MVP _obj_ ( _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok, seg_ ), with _λ_ equal to 0.1, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 10.0. For MVP _pair_ , we consider MVP _pair_ ( _char, seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ ), the combination of _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ and char based vocab, with _λ_ equal to 0.1, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 10.0. Note that to maintain the consistency for non-Chinese tokens, the char based vocab in MVP _hier_ ( _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ ), MVP _obj_ ( _char, seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ ) and MVP _pair_ ( _char, seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ ) is derived from _seg tok_ by splitting Chinese characters of _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ tokens into characters, not exactly the same with _char_ .

For pretraining, whole word masking is adopted, and total 15% of the words (from CWS) in the corpus are masked, which are then tokenized into different tokens under different vocabs. For MVP _obj_ ( _char, seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ ) and MVP _pair_ ( _char, seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ ), 1/3 of the time only tokens _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ are predicted, and 1/3 of the time only tokens from the derived char based vocab are predicted, and for the rest of the time, tokens from both vocabs are predicted.

In this article, all models use the ALBERT as encoder. We make use of a smaller parameter settings, that is, the number of layer is 3, the embedding size is 128 and the hidden size is 256. Other ALBERT configurations remain the same with ALBERT Lan et al. (2019). The pretraining hyper-parameters are almost the same with ALBERT Lan et al. (2019). The maximum sequence length is 512. Here, the sequence length is counted under the coarse-grained vocab for MVP _hier_ , fine-grained vocab for MVP _obj_ , and the longer one of the two sequences under the two vocabs for MVP _pair_ . The batch size is 1024, and all the model are trained for 12.5k steps. The pretraining optimizer is LAMB and the learning rate is 1e-4. For finetuning, the sequence length is 256, the learning rate is 2e-5, the optimizer is Adam Kingma & Ba (2015) and the batch size is set as the power of 2 and so that each epoch contains less than 1000 steps. Each model is run on a given task for 10 times and the average performance scores and standard deviations are reported for reproducibility.

## 4.2 BENCHMARK TASKS

For downstream tasks, we select 4 text classification tasks: (1) ChnSentiCorp (chn)[6] , a hotel review dataset; (2) Book review[7] (book ~~r~~ eview), collected from Douban[8] by Liu et al. (2019a). For sentence pair classification tasks, we include the following 4 datasets: (1) XNLI (xnli) from Conneau et al. (2018) ; (2) LCQMC (lcqmc) Liu et al. (2018); (3) NLPCC-DBQA[9] (nlpcc ~~d~~ bqa), a question-answer matching task in the open domain; (4) Law QA Liu et al. (2019a), a QA matching task in the legal domain. We also investigate three NER tasks. MSRA NER (msra) Levow (2006) is from open

## 4.3 EXPERIMENTAL RESULTS

We first compare the three single vocab models. We find that word based vocabs _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ and _seg_ perform better than _char_ on all sentence classification tasks, and _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ outperforms _seg_ even though having less parameters. However, _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ is worse than or comparable to _char_ on three NER tasks, where these performance gaps are only partially explained by the CWS errors[12] . We believe that its disadvantages on sequence labeling tasks is due to sparcity of tokens, i.e., each token has less training samples, thus are less well trained for token level classification. From the above results, we show that both CWS and subword tokenization are important to improve the expressiveness of Chinese PLMs in sentence level tasks via vocab re-designing. In addition, _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ is more efficient than _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ . we can observe a 1.35x speed up when changing the vocab from _char_ to _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ .

The lower rows of Table 1 also demonstrates the effectiveness of MVP training. Note that in this table, we report MVP _obj_ with _λ_ = 2 _._ 0, and MVP _pair_ with _λ_ = 1 _._ 0. First, we can see MVPs consistently improve the models’ performances, among which the largest improvements come from MVP _obj_ and ensemble MVP _pair_ . Second, for _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ , keeping only one encoder provides improvements on sequence labeling tasks, but the improvements are less than that provided by MVP _pair_ ( _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok, seg_ ) on sentence level tasks. Third, note that for NER tasks, ensemble MVP _pair_ performs better than single MVP _pair_ , and coarse-to-fine ensemble is more effective than fine-to-coarse ensemble. Fourth, MVP _obj_ ( _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok, seg_ ) achieve performances that are close to MVP _pair_ ( _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok, char, e_ ) on lcqmc, with less than half of the time in pre-training and fine-tuning. But the model’s improvements on NER tasks are less significant. Notice that for pretraining and finetuning, MVP _pair_ takes almost twice the resources and time than MVP _obj_ . Thus, for resources limited scenarios, MVP _obj_ could be a more suitable choice.

## 4.4 EFFECTS OF _λ_

In this section, we investigate how the relative importance coefficient _λ_ for MVP _pair_ ( _char, seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ ) affects the ALBERT models’ downstream performances. Here for MVP _pair_ , _λ_ is assigned to the loss term from coarse-grained vocab. We set _λ_ equal to 0.1, 0.5, 1, 2, 10, and run per-

For ensemble MVP _pair_ ( _char, seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok, e_ ), _λ_ = 1 _._ 0 provides the best performances. And for MVP _pair_ ( _char, seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok, s_ ), increasing _λ_ worsen the performance on NER, but not on sentence level tasks, and vice versa for MVP _pair_ ( _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok, char, s_ ).

## 5 CONCLUSIONS

In this work, we first propose a novel method, _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ , to re-build the vocabulary of Chinese BERT, with the help of Chinese word segmentation (CWS) and subword tokenization. Then we propose three versions of multi-vocabulary pretraining (MVP) to improve the models’ performance. Experiments show that: (a) compared with char based vocabulary, _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ does not only improves the performances of Chinese PLMs on sentence level tasks, it can also improve efficiency; (b) MVP improves PLMs’ downstream performance, especially it can improve _seg_ ~~_t_~~ _ok_ ’s performances on sequence labeling tasks.

coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Coarse (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback:
The paper addresses an important problem in Chinese NLP—vocabulary design for pre-trained language models—and proposes a novel hybrid tokenization method (seg_tok) along with three multi-vocabulary pretraining (MVP) strategies. The core idea of leveraging both subword and word-level information through segmentation-aware tokenization and multi-granularity objectives is well-motivated and aligns with known trade-offs in Chinese language modeling. The empirical evaluation covers a reasonable set of tasks and models, showing consistent gains from the proposed methods, particularly on sentence classification.

Overview issues:

- Inadequate ablation isolates seg_tok from MVP contributions: The paper fails to disentangle the individual contributions of seg_tok and the MVP framework. While results in Table 1 show improvements from MVP variants, there is no ablation that tests MVP strategies using the standard char vocabulary or applies seg_tok without any multi-vocabulary training. This makes it impossible to determine whether gains come from the new tokenization method, the multi-task pretraining setup, or their interaction. For example, one cannot tell if MVPobj(char, seg_tok) improves performance because of the auxiliary objective or simply due to better signal from seg_tok. A proper ablation would require testing MVPobj on char-only models or comparing seg_tok with and without MVPobj.

- Potential data leakage via jieba in pretraining and evaluation: The use of jieba for Chinese word segmentation during vocabulary construction raises concerns about data leakage, especially since jieba’s internal dictionary may overlap with named entities or domain-specific terms in downstream NER datasets like MSRA, Finance, or CCKS. If jieba was trained or tuned on data overlapping with these benchmarks, the model could gain unfair advantage by memorizing segmentation patterns. The paper does not report whether jieba was used in its default configuration or if its dictionary was modified, nor does it evaluate segmentation accuracy on out-of-domain text. This undermines the validity of NER results, particularly since seg_tok underperforms on these tasks and the authors attribute this to sparsity rather than potential leakage artifacts.

- Unjustified asymmetry in MVP_pair and inconsistent notation: The paper defines MVP_pair(V1, V2, i, j) but tests both MVP_pair(char, seg_tok, s) and MVP_pair(seg_tok, char, s), implying directionality matters. However, no justification is given for why one direction should outperform the other, nor is there analysis of how parameter sharing flows between encoders. Moreover, Figure 2(c) and Section 3.2 suggest symmetric training, yet results differ between the two orders. This asymmetry contradicts the claim of parameter sharing unless initialization or masking biases exist. Additionally, the notation MVP_pair(V1, V2, i, j) is inconsistently applied—some entries in Table 1 omit j when i=s, but others include it, creating confusion about model configurations.

- Missing comparison with modern baselines: The paper positions Google Chinese BERT’s char-based vocabulary as the dominant standard but does not compare against more recent and widely adopted Chinese PLMs such as WuDaoCorpora-based models, Chinese RoBERTa, or ModernBERT, which already incorporate whole-word masking and optimized vocabularies. This omission weakens the relevance of the claimed improvements. For instance, gains from seg_tok may be comparable to or smaller than those achieved by whole-word masking alone, as shown in Cui et al. (2019a). Without benchmarking against these stronger baselines, the contribution appears incremental rather than transformative.

- Efficiency claims lack full computational accounting: While the paper claims seg_tok improves efficiency with a 1.35x speedup over char, this appears to refer only to sequence length reduction and not end-to-end training or inference time. For MVP_pair, the authors acknowledge nearly double the pretraining and finetuning time but do not report actual wall-clock times, GPU-hours, or memory usage. Similarly, the efficiency of MVPobj is praised, but no breakdown of computational cost per step is provided. Given that ALBERT already uses parameter sharing, the real efficiency gain of MVPobj over MVP_pair may be smaller than implied. A full efficiency analysis should include throughput (samples/sec), memory footprint, and convergence speed.

- Hyperparameter sensitivity underexplored despite λ analysis: Although Figure 3 examines λ for MVP_pair, the analysis is limited to two tasks and does not extend to MVPobj or MVP_hier. More importantly, the optimal λ differs across tasks and model variants, yet the paper reports final results using fixed values (λ=2.0 for MVPobj, λ=1.0 for MVP_pair) without justification. This raises concerns about cherry-picking or overfitting to development sets. No ablation shows performance variance across λ on held-out test sets, nor is there discussion of how λ should be selected in practice. Given that λ controls the trade-off between fine- and coarse-grained objectives, its impact deserves more thorough investigation.

- No ablation of seg_tok without MVP: The paper claims that seg_tok improves performance on sentence-level tasks, but all reported results for seg_tok are either standalone or combined with MVP strategies, without showing seg_tok's performance when trained with standard MLM. To isolate the contribution of the vocabulary design itself, the authors must report results for a model using seg_tok vocabulary with standard single-vocabulary pretraining. Without this, it is impossible to determine whether gains attributed to seg_tok are actually due to implicit multi-granularity signals introduced during pretraining via whole-word masking or other factors. Add a row in Table 1 for 'seg_tok (vanilla MLM)' trained and evaluated under the same conditions as char.

- Missing comparison to whole-word masking baseline: The paper proposes seg_tok and MVP as advances over char-based BERT, but does not compare against the now-standard whole-word masking (WWM) extension for Chinese BERT, which already incorporates word-level information during pretraining. Since WWM is known to improve Chinese PLMs significantly, the claimed benefits of seg_tok and MVP may be marginal in comparison. The authors should include a 'char + WWM' baseline trained on the same corpus and evaluated on all tasks. This is standard practice in the literature (e.g., Cui et al., 2019a) and necessary to establish the novelty and impact of the proposed methods.

- No analysis of vocabulary coverage and OOV rates: The paper introduces seg_tok as a hybrid of CWS and subword tokenization but provides no quantitative analysis of how it balances known words and subword units. Readers need to know the percentage of tokens in downstream tasks that are OOV under char, seg, and seg_tok, as well as the distribution of subword splits in seg_tok. For example, compute the OOV rate of seg_tok on MSRA and CCKS NER datasets and compare it to char and seg. This is critical for interpreting performance differences, especially since the authors attribute seg_tok's weakness on NER to token sparsity—this claim requires empirical support.

- Inference efficiency not quantified for MVP variants: The paper claims MVP_obj is more efficient than MVP_pair, but only states that MVP_pair takes 'almost twice the resources' without reporting concrete metrics like inference latency, memory usage, or FLOPs. For a fair comparison, the authors should report average inference time per sample across tasks for MVP_obj, MVP_pair (single), and MVP_pair (ensemble), using identical hardware and batch sizes. This is essential for assessing practical applicability, particularly since the paper positions MVP_obj as a resource-friendly alternative.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Conduct and report a full ablation study that separates the effects of seg_tok and MVP by testing MVP strategies on char-based models and seg_tok without multi-vocabulary training.

- Address potential data leakage by either using a transparent, fixed CWS tool with documented training data or evaluating segmentation accuracy on out-of-domain text and reporting jieba’s configuration.

- Clarify the asymmetry in MVP_pair results by analyzing training dynamics, providing consistent notation, and justifying directional choices in model design.

- Include comparisons with modern Chinese PLM baselines that use whole-word masking or advanced tokenization to better contextualize the novelty and magnitude of improvements.

- Provide complete efficiency metrics including wall-clock training time, GPU memory usage, and throughput for all models, especially MVP variants.

comments.title: Incorrect notation for MVP_obj in definition
comments.quote: We will denote this strategy as  $\mathrm{MVP}_{pair}(V_1, V_2)$ , where  $V_1$  and  $V_2$  are a pair of vocab and  $V_1$  is the more fine-grained one.
comments.explanation: The strategy described is clearly $\mathrm{MVP}_{obj}$, as introduced in the preceding paragraph and illustrated in Figure 2(b), yet the authors use the notation $\mathrm{MVP}_{pair}(V_1, V_2)$. This is a direct notational error that risks confusion, especially since $\mathrm{MVP}_{pair}$ refers to a distinct dual-encoder architecture. The correct notation should be $\mathrm{MVP}_{obj}(V_1, V_2)$, with $V_1$ as the fine-grained vocabulary and $V_2$ as the coarse-grained one. This mistake undermines clarity in a core methodological definition.

comments.title: Inconsistent frequency propagation in seg vocab construction
comments.quote: c) for long tail Chinese words and non-Chinese tokens, tokenize them into subword tokens with seg_tok, and the words’ frequencies are added to the sub-word tokens’ frequencies; d) sort the vocab via frequency, and if the most frequent N words or tokens can cover 99.95% of the corpus, then take them as vocab. Note that some of the tokens from seg_tok will be dropped.
comments.explanation: There is a logical flaw in the seg vocabulary construction: step (c) aggregates word frequencies into subword tokens from seg_tok, but step (d) allows those same subword tokens to be dropped if they fall below the frequency threshold. This breaks the coverage guarantee—if a subword token used to decompose rare words is later removed, future instances of those words cannot be tokenized, leading to OOV errors. The procedure must preserve all seg_tok subword units to maintain compositional integrity. Otherwise, the 99.95% coverage claim is invalid.

comments.title: Ambiguity in seg_tok tokenization pipeline
comments.quote: seg_tok requires the sentences in corpus to be segmented and a sub-word tokenizer like BPE are learned on the segmented sentences. Note that in seg_tok, some natural word will be splitted into pieces, but there are still many tokens that have multiple Chinese chars.
comments.explanation: The description fails to specify whether BPE operates over segmented words as atomic units or over characters within those words. If BPE can split segmented words (e.g., '北京大学' → '北', '京', '大学'), then CWS provides only soft boundaries and may not preserve semantic units. Conversely, if BPE merges across word boundaries, it creates non-words. The interaction between CWS and subword tokenization must be explicitly defined—specifically, whether BPE respects word boundaries or not—to ensure reproducibility and meaningful tokenization.

comments.title: No handling of out-of-vocabulary compositionality in seg
comments.quote: d) sort the vocab via frequency, and if the most frequent N words or tokens can cover 99.95% of the corpus, then take them as vocab. Note that some of the tokens from seg_tok will be dropped.
comments.explanation: The seg vocabulary construction assumes rare words can be decomposed using seg_tok subword units, but if those units are dropped due to low frequency, decomposition fails entirely. This creates a fragility: the system relies on subword units that may not survive the frequency cutoff. There is no fallback mechanism (e.g., character-level splitting) described, nor is there a guarantee that seg_tok units are preserved. Without such safeguards, the tokenization process is not robust to vocabulary truncation and may produce untokenizable words.

comments.title: Missing specification of frequency initialization for seg_tok tokens
comments.quote: b) count the words’ frequency, and add the tokens from seg_tok with frequency 0;
comments.explanation: Initializing seg_tok tokens with frequency 0 creates a circular dependency: these tokens must be present to receive frequency via decomposition of rare words in step (c), but they are likely to be excluded in step (d) due to zero frequency. This undermines their availability for subword decomposition. To resolve this, the procedure should assign a minimal pseudo-count (e.g., frequency 1) to all seg_tok tokens to ensure they are retained and available for composition.

comments.title: Incorrect loss function for MVP_pair
comments.quote:
The loss function for MVP_{pair} is

$\min_{\theta}-\log\mathrm{P}_{\theta}(\mathbf{x}^{mask},\mathbf{y}^{mask}|\mathbf{x},\mathbf{y})\approx\min_{\theta}-\sum_{i=1}^{l_{x}}\mathrm{I}_{i}^{x}\log\mathrm{P}_{\theta}(x_{i}^{mask}|\mathbf{x})-\lambda*\sum_{i=1}^{l_{y}}\mathrm{I}_{i}^{y}\log\mathrm{P}_{\theta}(y_{i}^{mask}|\mathbf{y}),$
comments.explanation: The loss function incorrectly conditions each prediction on its own input sequence, suggesting independent models. However, the method uses parameter sharing, meaning the same $\theta$ processes both inputs. The correct formulation should reflect shared parameters: $\min_{\theta} -\sum_{i=1}^{l_{x}}\mathrm{I}_{i}^{x}\log\mathrm{P}_{\theta}(x_{i}^{mask}|\mathbf{x}) - \lambda \sum_{i=1}^{l_{y}}\mathrm{I}_{i}^{y}\log\mathrm{P}_{\theta}(y_{i}^{mask}|\mathbf{y})$, with $\theta$ shared. The current form misleadingly implies separate parameter sets, contradicting the core mechanism of knowledge transfer via shared weights.

comments.title: Ambiguous aggregator definition in MVP_hier
comments.quote:
Then representations of chars inside a word is aggregated into the representation of this word, which is further fed into the transformer encoder. During MLM task, whole word masking is applied, that is, we will mask 15% of the tokens

<!-- PAGE BREAK -->

in the $V_{c}$. For example in Figure 2(a), ”_喜欢” (like) is masked, thus in the char sequence, two tokens ”喜” and ”欢” are masked. Then an aggregator will combine the embeddings into the vectors of word tokens. At the MLM task, and
comments.explanation: The description contradicts itself: it states that character representations are aggregated *before* the transformer encoder, yet the example shows masking at the character level and aggregation *after* encoding. If aggregation happens before the encoder, the model operates on word-level inputs; if after, it must process characters. The example suggests the latter, but the initial sentence implies the former. This ambiguity affects reproducibility and must be clarified—likely by stating that the transformer processes character-level embeddings and aggregation occurs post-encoding for prediction.

comments.title: Inconsistent char vocabulary definition
comments.quote: Note that to maintain the consistency for non-Chinese tokens, the char based vocab in  $\mathrm{MVP}_{hier}(seg\_tok)$ ,  $\mathrm{MVP}_{obj}(char, seg\_tok)$  and  $\mathrm{MVP}_{pair}(char, seg\_tok)$  is derived from  $seg\_tok$  by splitting Chinese characters of  $seg\_tok$  tokens into characters, not exactly the same with char.
comments.explanation: The paper uses two different 'char' vocabularies: one standalone (size 21128) and another derived from seg_tok. This introduces a confound—performance differences attributed to MVP strategies may stem from differences in tokenization, especially for non-Chinese content. The derived char vocab inherits seg_tok's coverage and may split multi-character units differently. For fair comparison, all models should use the same char vocabulary, or the authors must report and justify any differences in token coverage and OOV rates.

comments.title: Ambiguous masking protocol for MVP variants
comments.quote: For  $\mathrm{MVP}_{obj}(char, seg\_tok)$  and  $\mathrm{MVP}_{pair}(char, seg\_tok)$ , 1/3 of the time only tokens  $seg\_tok$  are predicted, and 1/3 of the time only tokens from the derived char based vocab are predicted, and for the rest of the time, tokens from both vocab are predicted.
comments.explanation: The masking procedure does not specify how word-level masking from CWS is projected into the two tokenizations. Since seg_tok and derived char may split words differently, it is unclear whether masked spans are consistently aligned across views. Without synchronized masking—where a masked word leads to corresponding tokens being masked in both sequences—there is a risk of inconsistent supervision. The paper must clarify that masking is applied once at the word level and propagated to both token sequences to ensure aligned targets.

comments.title: Unjustified sequence length for MVP_pair
comments.quote: Here, the sequence length is counted under the coarse-grained vocab for  $\mathrm{MVP}_{hier}$ , fine-grained vocab for  $\mathrm{MVP}_{obj}$ , and the longer one of the two sequences under the two vocabals for  $\mathrm{MVP}_{pair}$ .
comments.explanation: Using the longer of two sequences for $\mathrm{MVP}_{pair}$ introduces a truncation bias: the shorter-sequence input may lose content if the other view has more tokens. For example, if char produces more tokens than seg_tok, the seg_tok representation may be cut off prematurely. The paper does not report which vocabulary typically determines length or how much text is lost. This choice affects context utilization and efficiency claims and should be justified with empirical statistics or alternative designs.

comments.title: Missing justification for seg_tok vocabulary size
comments.quote: The vocab size is 21128 for char and seg_tok.  $50.38\%$  of seg are Chinese tokens with length more than 1.
comments.explanation: The paper sets seg_tok's vocab size to 21128 without justification, unlike seg, which uses size 69341 to cover 99.95% of the corpus. Given that seg_tok combines CWS and subword tokenization, it is unclear whether this size achieves sufficient coverage. Without reporting corpus coverage or OOV rate for seg_tok, the comparison with char is weakened—gains could stem from better coverage rather than tokenization design. The authors should report these metrics to validate the choice.

comments.title: Incorrect NER metric labeling in table
comments.quote:
|  task | chn | br | lcqmc | xnli | nlpcc | msra | fin | ccks  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  metric | acc | acc | acc | acc | macro F1 | exact F1 | exact F1 | exact F1  |
comments.explanation: The table uses 'exact F1' for NER tasks, a non-standard and ambiguous term. In NER, strict boundary and type matching is conventionally reported as 'F1' with clarification in text. The term 'exact F1' is not widely recognized and may confuse readers. The label should be changed to 'F1' or 'F1 (strict)', with a note in the caption specifying the evaluation scheme to align with standard practice.

comments.title: Self-contradictory efficiency claim in seg_tok
comments.quote: seg_tok is more efficient than seg_tok. we can observe a  $1.35\mathrm{x}$  speed up when changing the vocab from char to seg_tok.
comments.explanation: The sentence 'seg_tok is more efficient than seg_tok' is logically incoherent—it claims a method outperforms itself. The intended comparison was clearly between seg_tok and char, as supported by the 1.35x speedup claim. This typo undermines clarity in a key result. The sentence should be corrected to 'seg_tok is more efficient than char' to reflect the actual comparison.

comments.title: Inconsistent notation for MVP_pair in text vs. table
comments.quote: Fourth,  $\mathrm{MVP}_{obj}(seg\_tok, seg)$  achieve performances that are close to  $\mathrm{MVP}_{pair}(seg\_tok, char, e)$  on lcqmc, with less than half of the time in pre-training and fine-tuning.
comments.explanation: The notation $\mathrm{MVP}_{pair}(seg\_tok, char, e)$ uses 'e' to denote ensemble, but this is not defined in the formal notation from Section 3.2, which uses i and j. This inconsistency creates ambiguity: is 'e' equivalent to i=e? The paper must standardize notation across text and tables, defining 'e' explicitly as an ensemble flag to ensure reproducibility.

comments.title: Performance trends lack statistical significance reporting
comments.quote: For ensemble  $\mathrm{MVP}_{pair}(char, seg\_tok, e)$ ,  $\lambda = 1.0$  provides the best performances. And for  $\mathrm{MVP}_{pair}(char, seg\_tok, s)$ , increasing  $\lambda$  worsen the performance on NER, but not on sentence level tasks, and vice versa for  $\mathrm{MVP}_{pair}(seg\_tok, char, s)$ .
comments.explanation: The paper makes definitive claims about optimal $\lambda$ values and performance trends, but no confidence intervals or significance tests are reported. Given the small margins in Figure 3, sampling variance could explain the observed differences. To support causal claims, the authors must report standard deviations and indicate whether differences are statistically significant, particularly around the claimed optimum.

comments.title: Typographical error in MVPobj(seg_tok, seg) results
comments.explanation: The entry for MVPobj(seg_tok, seg) on MSRA contains a trailing period: '81.05.'. This is a clear formatting error not present elsewhere in the table and risks parsing failures or misinterpretation. The value should be '81.05' to maintain consistency with all other numerical entries.

coarse__glm-4.6.label: Coarse (glm-4.6)
coarse__glm-4.6.overall_feedback:
This paper proposes seg_tok (combining Chinese word segmentation with subword tokenization) and three Multi-Vocab Pretraining (MVP) strategies to improve Chinese BERT. While the ideas are reasonable and experiments show some gains, the paper suffers from significant methodological gaps, incomplete comparisons with state-of-the-art, and insufficient analysis of the trade-offs involved.

The paper addresses an important problem in Chinese NLP - the limitations of character-based vocabularies - and proposes novel solutions. The seg_tok approach is sensible, and the MVP strategies represent an interesting direction for multi-granularity learning. However, the experimental evaluation is incomplete, the model architecture choices are poorly justified, and critical comparisons with recent competing work are missing, undermining the paper's claimed contributions.

Overview issues:

- Incomplete comparison with state-of-the-art Chinese BERT variants: The paper fails to compare against recent Chinese BERT variants that directly address the same problems. Notably absent are comparisons with Whole Word Masking BERT (Cui et al. 2019), which incorporates word-level information during pretraining, and MarkBERT (Zhang et al. 2021), which explicitly marks word boundaries. These are the most relevant baselines for evaluating seg_tok and MVP on sequence labeling tasks. Without these comparisons, it's impossible to determine whether the proposed methods offer genuine improvements over existing solutions or merely replicate similar benefits through different means.

- Unexplained architectural choices and scalability concerns: The paper uses ALBERT base configuration (3 layers, 256 hidden size) rather than standard BERT or larger models, but provides no justification. This raises questions about whether the results generalize to modern-scale models. More critically, the MVP strategies significantly increase computational requirements - MVP_pair doubles pretraining time and memory - yet the paper provides no analysis of the performance-cost tradeoff. For MVP_pair ensemble models, inference costs would be prohibitive in practice, but this limitation is never acknowledged. The paper should include efficiency benchmarks (tokens/sec, memory usage) and discuss scalability.

- Inadequate ablation study of seg_tok components: The seg_tok method combines CWS with subword tokenization, but the paper doesn't disentangle their contributions. We need to know: (1) How much does CWS alone contribute vs. subword tokenization? (2) What's the impact of CWS errors on downstream performance? (3) How sensitive is seg_tok to the choice of CWS tool? The paper mentions CWS errors affect NER performance (Section 4.3) but provides no quantitative analysis. Without proper ablation, we cannot understand why seg_tok helps sentence-level tasks but hurts sequence labeling.

- Limited evaluation scope and missing critical analysis: The evaluation is restricted to Wikipedia pretraining and standard benchmarks, but doesn't test: (1) Domain adaptation scenarios where vocab design might matter more; (2) Low-resource settings where OOV handling is crucial; (3) Generation tasks where the paper claims word-based models reduce exposure bias. Additionally, the MVP_obj results show inconsistent behavior across tasks (good on sentence tasks, weak on NER) but this isn't analyzed. The paper needs to investigate why MVP strategies help certain tasks but not others, and test in more diverse scenarios to demonstrate robustness.

- Insufficient technical details for reproducibility: Critical implementation details are missing: (1) The exact CWS tool version and settings; (2) How non-Chinese tokens are handled across different vocabularies (Section 4.1 mentions they're "kept the same" but this is ambiguous); (3) The aggregation method for character embeddings in MVP_hier; (4) Hyperparameter search details for λ in MVP strategies. Without these specifics, reproducing the results would be difficult, especially the complex MVP architectures where small implementation differences could significantly impact performance.

- No demonstration of seg_tok vocabulary construction: The paper introduces seg_tok as a novel vocabulary method combining CWS and subword tokenization, but never shows a concrete example of how it processes text. Readers need to see exactly how seg_tok tokenizes sample sentences, how it differs from char and seg, and what the resulting vocabulary looks like. Without this worked example, it's impossible to understand the method's mechanics or evaluate its claimed benefits. The paper should include a detailed walkthrough of seg_tok processing several representative Chinese sentences, showing the intermediate CWS output, subword tokenization steps, and final tokens.

- Missing computational efficiency analysis: The paper claims seg_tok improves efficiency and that MVP_pair has higher inference time, but provides no quantitative measurements. For a method that redesigns vocabularies and pretraining, readers need concrete benchmarks: tokens processed per second, memory usage during pretraining/finetuning, and inference latency comparisons. The claim of 1.35x speedup for seg_tok needs context - is this measured during pretraining or inference? On what hardware? The paper should include systematic efficiency measurements across all methods, especially since MVP strategies significantly increase computational costs.

- No analysis of CWS error propagation: The paper mentions CWS errors affect NER performance but provides no quantitative investigation. Since seg_tok relies on CWS, understanding error propagation is critical. The paper should analyze: (1) CWS error rates on their pretraining corpus; (2) How specific CWS error types impact downstream tasks; (3) Comparison with alternative CWS tools. Without this analysis, readers cannot assess seg_tok's robustness or determine whether performance limitations are inherent to the approach or due to CWS quality.

- Missing comparison with word masking baselines: The paper fails to compare against Whole Word Masking BERT (Cui et al. 2019), which directly addresses the same problem of incorporating word-level information in Chinese BERT. This is the most relevant baseline since MVP strategies also operate at word level. The paper should include direct comparisons with word masking variants on the same tasks to demonstrate whether MVP provides additional benefits beyond established techniques. Without this comparison, the contribution of MVP over existing word-aware pretraining methods remains unclear.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Add comparisons with Whole Word Masking BERT and MarkBERT as primary baselines

- Include efficiency analysis (speed/memory) and scalability discussion for all methods

- Conduct comprehensive ablation study of seg_tok components and CWS error analysis

- Expand evaluation to include domain adaptation, low-resource, and text generation tasks

- Provide complete implementation details and hyperparameter search procedures

comments.title: Notation inconsistency in MVP_obj definition
comments.quote: The third version of MVP (denoted as  $\mathrm{MVP}_{obj}$ ) is depicted in Figure 2(b). In  $\mathrm{MVP}_{obj}$ , the sentence is encoded only once with a fine-grained vocab, and MLM task with that vocab is conducted. As in the figure, the word "喜欢" (like) is masked, and under the vocab of char, the PLM has to predict "喜" and "欢" for the two masked tokens. As additional training objective, we will employ a more coarse-grained vocab like seg_tok and ask the model to use the starting token ("喜")'s representation to predict the original word under seg_tok. We will denote this strategy as  $\mathrm{MVP}_{pair}(V_1, V_2)$ , where  $V_1$  and  $V_2$  are a pair of vocab and  $V_1$  is the more fine-grained one.
comments.explanation: The text introduces MVP_obj but then incorrectly refers to it as MVP_pair at the end. Since MVP_pair is already defined as a different strategy, this creates confusion. The final sentence should use MVP_obj notation consistently: "We will denote this strategy as MVP_obj(V_1, V_2), where V_1 and V_2 are a pair of vocab and V_1 is the more fine-grained one."

comments.title: Logical contradiction in seg vocabulary construction
comments.quote: seg with size $N$ is built with the following procedures: a) do CWS on the corpus; b) count the words’ frequency, and add the tokens from seg_tok with frequency 0; c) for long tail Chinese words and non-Chinese tokens, tokenize them into subword tokens with seg_tok, and the words’ frequencies are added to the sub-word tokens’ frequencies; d) sort the vocab via frequency, and if the most frequent N words or tokens can cover 99.95% of the corpus, then take them as vocab. Note that some of the tokens from seg_tok will be dropped.
comments.explanation: The procedure contains a logical inconsistency. Step (b) adds seg_tok tokens with frequency 0, but step (c) adds frequencies from long tail words to seg_tok subword tokens. This means some seg_tok tokens start with frequency 0 while others accumulate positive frequencies. Step (d) then sorts by frequency and drops tokens, but it's unclear how tokens with frequency 0 could survive this sorting while remaining meaningful. The authors need to clarify whether seg_tok tokens start with frequency 0 and accumulate frequencies from step (c), or if there's a different mechanism for handling their initial frequencies.

comments.title: MVP_hier loss function conditioning issue
comments.quote:
Let $\mathbf{x}$ and $\mathbf{y}$ denote the sequences of tokens with length $l_{x}$ and $l_{y}$, for the same sentence under $V_{c}$ and $V_{f}$, in which a part of tokens are masked. Denote $\mathbf{x}^{mask}$ as the masked tokens under $V_{c}$. The loss function for $\mathrm{MVP}_{hier}$ is

$\min_{\theta}-\log\mathrm{P}_{\theta}(\mathbf{x}^{mask}|\mathbf{x},\mathbf{y})\approx\min_{\theta}-\sum_{i=1}^{l_{x}}\mathrm{I}_{i}^{x}\log\mathrm{P}_{\theta}(x_{i}^{mask}|\mathbf{x},\mathbf{y}),$
comments.explanation: The loss function conditions on both $\mathbf{x}$ and $\mathbf{y}$ when predicting masked tokens, but $\mathbf{y}$ is derived from $\mathbf{x}$ (character sequence from word sequence). This creates redundancy. The conditioning should be $\mathrm{P}_{\theta}(\mathbf{x}^{mask}|\mathbf{x}^{unmask})$ where $\mathbf{x}^{unmask}$ are the unmasked tokens from $V_c$. Rewrite the loss as $\min_{\theta}-\sum_{i=1}^{l_{x}}\mathrm{I}_{i}^{x}\log\mathrm{P}_{\theta}(x_{i}^{mask}|\mathbf{x}^{unmask})$ because the model should only use unmasked tokens for prediction.

comments.title: MVP_obj loss function conditioning error
comments.quote:
The loss function for $\mathrm{MVP}_{obj}$ is as follows:

$\min_{\theta}-\log\mathrm{P}_{\theta}(\mathbf{x}^{mask},\mathbf{y}^{mask}|\mathbf{x})\approx\min_{\theta}-\sum_{i=1}^{l_{x}}\mathrm{I}_{i}^{x}\log\mathrm{P}_{\theta}(x_{i}^{mask}|\mathbf{x})-\lambda*\sum_{i=1}^{l_{y}}\mathrm{I}_{i}^{y}\log\mathrm{P}_{\theta}(y_{i}^{mask}|\mathbf{x}),$
comments.explanation: The second term conditions on $\mathbf{x}$ when predicting $y_{i}^{mask}$ (coarse-grained tokens), but $\mathbf{x}$ is the fine-grained sequence. For the auxiliary MLM task on $V_c$, the prediction should condition on unmasked tokens from the coarse-grained sequence. Rewrite the loss as $\min_{\theta}-\sum_{i=1}^{l_{x}}\mathrm{I}_{i}^{x}\log\mathrm{P}_{\theta}(x_{i}^{mask}|\mathbf{x}^{unmask})-\lambda*\sum_{i=1}^{l_{y}}\mathrm{I}_{i}^{y}\log\mathrm{P}_{\theta}(y_{i}^{mask}|\mathbf{y}^{unmask})$ because each MLM task should use its corresponding unmasked tokens for prediction.

comments.title: MVP_pair loss function conditioning mismatch
comments.quote:
Formally, Thus the loss function for $\mathrm{MVP}_{pair}$ is

$\min_{\theta}-\log\mathrm{P}_{\theta}(\mathbf{x}^{mask},\mathbf{y}^{mask}|\mathbf{x},\mathbf{y})\approx\min_{\theta}-\sum_{i=1}^{l_{x}}\mathrm{I}_{i}^{x}\log\mathrm{P}_{\theta}(x_{i}^{mask}|\mathbf{x})-\lambda*\sum_{i=1}^{l_{y}}\mathrm{I}_{i}^{y}\log\mathrm{P}_{\theta}(y_{i}^{mask}|\mathbf{y}),$
comments.explanation: The joint probability conditions on both complete sequences but the approximation terms condition on individual sequences. Since the encoders are separate with shared parameters, each MLM task should only use its corresponding unmasked tokens. Rewrite as $\min_{\theta}-\sum_{i=1}^{l_{x}}\mathrm{I}_{i}^{x}\log\mathrm{P}_{\theta}(x_{i}^{mask}|\mathbf{x}^{unmask})-\lambda*\sum_{i=1}^{l_{y}}\mathrm{I}_{i}^{y}\log\mathrm{P}_{\theta}(y_{i}^{mask}|\mathbf{y}^{unmask})$ because this correctly reflects the separate encoder architecture described.

comments.title: Inconsistent vocabulary size description
comments.quote:
The vocab size is 21128 for char and seg_tok.  $50.38\%$  of seg are Chinese tokens with length more than 1.

In this article, we adopt jieba as our CWS tool. To keep  $70\%$  of the vocab as natural words from CWS, and cover  $99.95\%$  of the corpus, seg is built with vocab size 69341.
comments.explanation: The text states seg_tok has vocabulary size 21128, but then describes building seg with vocabulary size 69341. This creates confusion about whether seg_tok and seg are the same vocabulary or different ones. If they are different, the vocabulary construction method for seg_tok (21128 size) is not explained, only the construction method for seg (69341 size) is provided. The paper should clarify whether seg_tok refers to the 21128-size vocabulary or the 69341-size vocabulary, or explicitly state that these are two different vocabularies with different construction criteria.

comments.title: Ambiguous derived char vocabulary definition
comments.quote: Note that to maintain the consistency for non-Chinese tokens, the char based vocab in  $\mathrm{MVP}_{hier}(seg\_tok)$ ,  $\mathrm{MVP}_{obj}(char, seg\_tok)$  and  $\mathrm{MVP}_{pair}(char, seg\_tok)$  is derived from  $seg\_tok$  by splitting Chinese characters of  $seg\_tok$  tokens into characters, not exactly the same with char.
comments.explanation: The description of how the derived char vocabulary is created is ambiguous. If seg_tok contains both single characters and multi-character words, splitting Chinese characters would result in a vocabulary that contains all individual characters from seg_tok. However, this should be identical to the original char vocabulary (21128 size) if char contains all Chinese characters. The claim that it's "not exactly the same with char" needs clarification - what specific differences exist? Are there characters in the original char vocab that don't appear in seg_tok, or vice versa? This affects the comparability of MVP results.

comments.title: Inconsistent sequence length counting for MVP_pair
comments.quote: Here, the sequence length is counted under the coarse-grained vocab for  $\mathrm{MVP}_{hier}$ , fine-grained vocab for  $\mathrm{MVP}_{obj}$ , and the longer one of the two sequences under the two vocabals for  $\mathrm{MVP}_{pair}$ .
comments.explanation: The sequence length counting for MVP_pair is problematic. If the maximum sequence length is 512, and MVP_pair uses "the longer one of the two sequences under the two vocabals", this could lead to inconsistent training. For example, if a text has 400 characters but only 250 seg_tok tokens, using the longer sequence (400) would mean the char sequence exceeds the seg_tok sequence length. This creates an imbalance in the 1/3 prediction scheme described earlier, where both vocabularies should be predicted simultaneously. The paper should clarify how this length discrepancy is handled during the joint prediction phases.

comments.title: Missing data for MVP_pair configuration
comments.explanation: The table shows missing results (dashes) for the first 5 tasks under MVP_pair(char, seg_tok, e, ctf) configuration, but provides results for the last 3 NER tasks. This creates an inconsistency in the experimental reporting. Either the missing results should be provided, or there should be an explanation in the text about why these specific tasks were not evaluated for this configuration. Add a footnote or explanation in the text to clarify why results are missing for these tasks.

comments.title: Undefined ensemble methods
comments.quote: and coarse-to-fine ensemble is more effective than fine-to-coarse ensemble.
comments.explanation: The paper mentions 'coarse-to-fine ensemble' and 'fine-to-coarse ensemble' without any prior definition or explanation of what these ensemble strategies entail. Readers cannot understand what distinguishes these approaches or why one would be more effective than the other. Define these ensemble methods before making comparative claims about their effectiveness.

comments.title: Lambda coefficient assignment inconsistency
comments.quote: Here for  $\mathrm{MVP}_{pair}$ ,  $\lambda$  is assigned to the loss term from coarse-grained vocab. We set  $\lambda$  equal to 0.1, 0.5, 1, 2, 10, and run per
comments.explanation: The text states λ is assigned to the coarse-grained vocab loss term, but later discusses MVP_pair(char, seg_tok) and MVP_pair(seg_tok, char) without clarifying whether λ is always assigned to the first argument or always to the actual coarse-grained vocab regardless of position. This ambiguity makes it impossible to interpret the experimental results correctly. Rewrite the sentence as "Here for $\mathrm{MVP}_{pair}$, $\lambda$ is assigned to the loss term from the first vocabulary argument (coarse-grained vocab in our experiments). We set $\lambda$ equal to 0.1, 0.5, 1, 2, 10, and run pretraining under each setting" because this resolves the ambiguity about λ assignment.

comments.title: Contradictory lambda effect description
comments.quote: And for  $\mathrm{MVP}_{pair}(char, seg\_tok, s)$ , increasing  $\lambda$  worsen the performance on NER, but not on sentence level tasks, and vice versa for  $\mathrm{MVP}_{pair}(seg\_tok, char, s)$ .
comments.explanation: The description contains a logical contradiction. If λ is always assigned to the coarse-grained vocab (char in first case, seg_tok in second), then the effects should be consistent, not opposite. The "vice versa" claim contradicts the earlier statement about λ assignment. Rewrite the sentence as "And for $\mathrm{MVP}_{pair}(char, seg\_tok, s)$, increasing $\lambda$ worsens the performance on NER, but not on sentence level tasks. For $\mathrm{MVP}_{pair}(seg\_tok, char, s)$, increasing $\lambda$ worsens sentence level tasks but not NER" because this removes the ambiguous "vice versa" and explicitly states the observed effects.

comments.title: Figure 2 reference inconsistency
comments.quote: Figure 2(a) depicts the architecture of $\mathrm{MVP}_{hier}$ and Figure 2(a) depicts its procedure for processing input sentences.
comments.explanation: The text references Figure 2(a) twice in the same sentence. The first reference should be to Figure 2(a) for architecture and the second should be to Figure 2(b) for the processing procedure. Rewrite the sentence as "Figure 2(a) depicts the architecture of $\mathrm{MVP}_{hier}$ and Figure 2(b) depicts its procedure for processing input sentences." because the current duplication creates ambiguity about which figure contains which information.

comments.title: Undefined aggregator method in MVP_hier
comments.quote: Then representations of chars inside a word is aggregated into the representation of this word, which is further fed into the transformer encoder.
comments.explanation: The text mentions aggregation but doesn't specify the method used in MVP_hier, while MVP_obj explicitly states using "BERT-style pooler". Add "For MVP_hier, we use mean pooling to aggregate character representations into word representations" after the quoted sentence because readers need to know the exact aggregation method to understand the model architecture.

comments.title: Incomplete notation definition
comments.quote: $\mathrm{MVP}_{obj}$ with a fine-grained vocab $V_{f}$ and a coarse-grained vocab $V_{c}$ are denoted
comments.explanation: The sentence defining notation for MVP_obj is incomplete due to page break. Add the missing completion "as $\mathrm{MVP}_{obj}(V_f, V_c)$" after the quoted text because the notation definition is essential for understanding subsequent references to this model variant.

comments.title: Efficiency comparison typo
comments.quote: In addition, seg_tok is more efficient than seg_tok. we can observe a  $1.35\mathrm{x}$  speed up when changing the vocab from char to seg_tok.
comments.explanation: There's a clear typo in the efficiency comparison sentence. The text states 'seg_tok is more efficient than seg_tok' which compares the same method to itself. Based on the context and the following clause about changing vocab from char to seg_tok, this should read 'seg_tok is more efficient than char'. Rewrite 'In addition, seg_tok is more efficient than seg_tok.' as 'In addition, seg_tok is more efficient than char.' because the comparison is intended to show the efficiency gain of seg_tok over the character-based baseline.

comments.title: Incomplete lambda value specification
comments.quote: We set  $\lambda$  equal to 0.1, 0.5, 1, 2, 10, and run per
comments.explanation: The sentence is incomplete, cutting off after "run per" without specifying what was run per each setting. This appears to be a sentence fragment that should have completed with something like "run experiments under each setting" or "run pretraining under each setting". Rewrite "We set $\lambda$ equal to 0.1, 0.5, 1, 2, 10, and run per" as "We set $\lambda$ equal to 0.1, 0.5, 1, 2, 10, and run pretraining under each setting" because this completes the sentence and clarifies the experimental procedure.

coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
This paper proposes 'seg_tok', a vocabulary construction method for Chinese BERT using word segmentation and subword tokenization, alongside three multi-vocabulary pre-training (MVP) strategies. The authors evaluate these methods on several Chinese NLP benchmarks, showing performance gains on sentence-level tasks and specific sequence labeling tasks.

The paper addresses a practical and well-motivated problem in Chinese language modeling: the limitations of character-based vocabularies. The proposed MVP strategies offer interesting architectural variations for multi-granularity learning. However, the experimental evaluation lacks necessary controls to distinguish between the effects of the new vocabulary and the effects of the multi-vocabulary training objectives.

Overview issues:

- Confounding of Vocabulary and Training Objective: The paper introduces both a new vocabulary (seg_tok) and new training objectives (MVP). In Table 1, it is difficult to determine if the performance gains for MVP models are due to the superior expressiveness of the multi-vocab approach or simply because the model is seeing a larger, more diverse set of tokens during pre-training. To isolate these effects, the authors should provide a baseline using the same total parameter count and pre-training data volume but with a single, unified vocabulary that matches the combined size of the multi-vocab setup. Without this, the claim that MVP improves expressiveness remains confounded by potential capacity differences.

- Inconsistent Handling of CWS Errors: The authors acknowledge that CWS errors impact downstream performance for seg_tok, yet the methodology relies on a single, off-the-shelf segmenter (jieba). Since segmentation quality varies significantly across domains—such as the difference between formal news and the social media text in the ChnSentiCorp dataset—the robustness of the seg_tok vocabulary is questionable. The paper should include an analysis of how segmentation error rates correlate with performance drops across the different benchmarks. Providing a sensitivity analysis or comparing performance using a more accurate, state-of-the-art segmenter would clarify if the observed gains are stable or dependent on the specific artifacts of jieba.

- Lack of Efficiency Trade-off Analysis: While the paper claims seg_tok improves efficiency, it does not provide a rigorous comparison of the memory footprint or inference latency for the MVP models. Given that MVP_pair requires two transformer encoders, the claim that it is a viable alternative to char-based BERT is incomplete without a clear discussion of the computational cost. The authors should include a table comparing the FLOPs, memory usage, and inference time per sentence for the baseline BERT versus the various MVP configurations. This is critical for practitioners who need to decide if the marginal performance gains justify the increased resource requirements.

- Ambiguity in Ensemble vs. Single Model Comparisons: In Section 4.3, the results for MVP_pair are presented for both single-encoder and ensemble configurations. It is unclear if the single model performance is compared against a baseline that has undergone the same amount of compute. If the ensemble model is significantly better, the paper should clarify whether this is due to the multi-vocab pre-training objective or simply the benefit of ensembling two models. A fairer comparison would be to compare the ensemble MVP_pair against an ensemble of two standard char-based BERT models trained for the same number of steps.

- Lack of controlled comparison for MVP performance gains: The paper claims that MVP strategies improve downstream performance, but the results in Table 1 do not isolate the benefits of the multi-vocabulary objective from potential gains due to increased parameter count or training complexity. For instance, comparing MVP_pair (which uses two encoders) against a baseline of two concatenated standard ALBERT encoders trained with the same total compute budget is necessary to verify if the multi-vocabulary approach provides a unique advantage. Without such a controlled baseline, the performance improvements could be attributed to model capacity rather than the proposed architectural innovation. I suggest adding a 'Double-Encoder Baseline' experiment where two standard character-based models are ensembled and trained for the same number of steps as MVP_pair to demonstrate the specific utility of the multi-vocab training objective.

- Absence of sensitivity analysis regarding CWS tool dependency: The proposed 'seg_tok' vocabulary relies entirely on the output of the jieba segmenter, which is known to produce inconsistent results across different domains. The current evaluation does not address whether the performance gains are robust to segmentation errors or if they are artifacts of the specific biases inherent in jieba. To demonstrate the generalizability of the seg_tok method, the paper should include a sensitivity analysis comparing the performance of the model when using at least one alternative, higher-accuracy CWS tool (such as PKUSEG or LTP). If the performance improvements diminish significantly with a different segmenter, the paper's claim that seg_tok is a robust vocabulary construction method would need to be qualified.

- Missing computational overhead and latency benchmarks: While the paper claims that seg_tok improves efficiency compared to character-based models, it provides no quantitative data on the memory footprint or inference latency for the more complex MVP models. Given that MVP_pair and MVP_hier introduce additional parameters and architectural complexity, practitioners need to know the cost of these improvements. I recommend adding a summary table that reports the number of parameters, FLOPs per forward pass, and average inference latency (in milliseconds per sentence) for the baseline ALBERT, seg_tok, and the three MVP variants. This data is essential for the paper to deliver on its implicit promise of providing practical, improved language models for real-world deployment.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Provide a controlled baseline experiment that compares the MVP models against a single-vocabulary model with equivalent parameter counts and training compute.

- Include a detailed efficiency analysis (latency and memory) for the ensemble MVP_pair models to justify their practical utility.

- Add an ablation study or discussion on the impact of CWS quality by testing the seg_tok method with different segmentation tools or by injecting synthetic segmentation noise.

- Clarify the comparison between the ensemble MVP_pair and an ensemble of standard BERT models to isolate the value added by multi-vocabulary pre-training.

comments.title: Missing experimental results
comments.quote:
4 EXPERIMENTS

# 4.1 Setu
comments.explanation: The section header for experiments is present, but the content is entirely missing. As this section is central to validating the claims regarding 'seg_tok' and 'MVP' performance, the absence of data, tables, and analysis makes it impossible to evaluate the paper's empirical contributions. Please provide the experimental results, including the performance metrics on downstream tasks and the necessary baseline comparisons.

comments.title: Inconsistent loss function definition for MVP_pair
comments.quote:
Formally, Thus the loss function for $\mathrm{MVP}_{pair}$ is

$\min_{\theta}-\log\mathrm{P}_{\theta}(\mathbf{x}^{mask},\mathbf{y}^{mask}|\mathbf{x},\mathbf{y})\approx\min_{\theta}-\sum_{i=1}^{l_{x}}\mathrm{I}_{i}^{x}\log\mathrm{P}_{\theta}(x_{i}^{mask}|\mathbf{x})-\lambda*\sum_{i=1}^{l_{y}}\mathrm{I}_{i}^{y}\log\mathrm{P}_{\theta}(y_{i}^{mask}|\mathbf{y}),$
comments.explanation: The loss function as written assumes conditional independence between the two masked language modeling tasks given their respective inputs. However, the text claims that self-supervised signals are transferred between vocabularies via parameter sharing. If the encoders share parameters $\theta$, the joint probability does not factorize into the product of independent marginals unless the parameters are decoupled. Please rewrite the loss function to explicitly show the joint dependency or clarify that the approximation assumes independence of the masked tokens given the shared parameters.

comments.title: Logical inconsistency in vocabulary construction
comments.quote: b) count the words’ frequency, and add the tokens from seg_tok with frequency 0; c) for long tail Chinese words and non-Chinese tokens, tokenize them into subword tokens with seg_tok, and the words’ frequencies are added to the sub-word tokens’ frequencies;
comments.explanation: The procedure described in steps (b) and (c) is contradictory. Step (b) initializes the frequency of all tokens from the 'seg_tok' vocabulary to 0. Step (c) then adds frequencies from 'long tail Chinese words' to these same tokens. If the tokens in 'seg_tok' are the same as the sub-word tokens used in step (c), their frequencies cannot be 0 at the start of step (c). Please clarify whether the 'seg_tok' vocabulary is a subset of the 'seg' vocabulary or if they are treated as independent entities during the frequency counting process.

comments.title: Inconsistent vocabulary size definition
comments.quote:
Wikipedia. The vocab size is 21128 for char and seg_tok.  $50.38\%$  of seg are Chinese tokens with length more than 1.

In this article, we adopt jieba as our CWS tool. To keep  $70\%$  of the vocab as natural words from CWS, and cover  $99.95\%$  of the corpus, seg is built with vocab size 69341.
comments.explanation: The text provides two conflicting definitions for the vocabulary size of the segmentation-based approach. The first sentence states the size is 21,128, while the second paragraph claims it is 69,341. Please clarify if these represent different experimental configurations or if one value is a typographical error.

comments.title: Misleading claim regarding CWS error impact
comments.quote: Second, it does not rely on CWS, thus avoiding word segmentation error, which can directly result in performance gain in span based tasks such as named entity recognition (NER).
comments.explanation: The claim that character-based models avoid CWS errors and thus gain performance on NER is misleading. While character-based models do not require explicit pre-segmentation, NER is a span-based task where the model must learn to identify boundaries. If a model fails to identify the correct span, it effectively makes a 'segmentation error' internally. Please rephrase this to clarify that character-based models avoid *pre-processing* segmentation errors, rather than avoiding segmentation errors entirely.

comments.title: Ambiguous description of lambda sensitivity
comments.quote: And for  $\mathrm{MVP}_{pair}(char, seg\_tok, s)$ , increasing  $\lambda$  worsen the performance on NER, but not on sentence level tasks, and vice versa for  $\mathrm{MVP}_{pair}(seg\_tok, char, s)$ .
comments.explanation: The 'vice versa' construction is logically underspecified. It is unclear if this implies that for the second configuration, increasing lambda worsens sentence-level tasks but not NER, or if it improves NER while worsening sentence-level tasks. Please explicitly state the performance trend for each task type under the second configuration instead of relying on the 'vice versa' shorthand.

comments.title: Inaccurate attribution of whole word masking
comments.quote: pre-training. Although not mentioned in *Devlin et al. (2018)*, the authors propose whole word masking in their open-source repository, which is effective for pretraining BERT.
